macro_segments.title: Protection of Third-Party Purchasers from Creditor Collection: The Core Tikkun Olam

micro_segments.title: The Rule: Creditors Cannot Collect from Liened Property Held by Third Parties When Debtor Has Available Assets
The Braita on Deathbed Distribution: Sequential vs. Equal Division of Estate
Creditor Collection Under Sequential Distribution: Why Protection Extends Even to Gifts
Reframing the Braita: Payoff of Debts Rather Than Gifts, and the Role of Documentary Evidence
Resolution: Sequential Priority Based on Declaration Document, Not Property Grade

macro_segments.title: Indeterminate and Unwritten Debts: The Rationale for Restricting Third-Party Collection

micro_segments.title: Setting the Stage: Daughters' Support and Wives' Ketuba Obligations as Indeterminate Liens
The Case of Stolen Land and Improvement Claims: Unknown Future Liability
Ulla and Reish Lakish: The Debt Is Unknown Because It Is Not Written
Tension Between Kesubah Obligations and Meshubadim Protection: Written vs. Indeterminate
Rabbi Chanina's Counterargument: The Real Issue Is That the Amount Is Not Fixed

macro_segments.title: Fixed Amounts Without Documents: Can Fixed Debts Collect from Third Parties?

micro_segments.title: The Question: Do Both Certainty and Documentation Matter, or Only One?
The Case of the Deceased with Two Daughters and an Inheriting Son: Dowry Liens
Rabbi Chanina's Assertion: Dowry (Parnasa) Is Collectible from Third Parties
Gemara's Response: Parnasa Is Special Because It Is So Well-Known It Is Treated As Written
The Stepdaughter Case: Fixed Duration Support with Kinyan Creates Collectible Debt
Gemara's Resolution: Kinyan Makes the Stepdaughter Special; Daughters' Kesubah Rights Differ

macro_segments.title: Capital Improvements and Stolen Property: When Fixed Amounts Allow Third-Party Collection

micro_segments.title: The Braita: Improvements Made Before the Subsequent Purchase Allow Collection
Tosafot's Reading: The Amount Being Fixed and Known Is What Matters
Gemara's Conclusion: The Debate Is Between Tannaim on Whether It's About Written or Fixed

macro_segments.title: The Finder of Lost Property: Tikkun Olam vs. The Opinion of Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov

micro_segments.title: The Principle: A Finder of Lost Property Is Exempt from Oath Due to Tikkun Olam
Rav Yitzchak's Cases: Two Wallets vs. Two Oxen and the Test of Natural Separation
Gemara's Challenge: Does Rav Yitzchak Follow Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov's Opinion?
Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov: A Person May Swear on Their Own Admission (Modeh B'Miktzat)
The Case of Admitting to Half a Father's Debt: Why Must an Admission Trigger an Oath?

macro_segments.title: The Debate on Admission vs. Silence: Migo and the Status of an Ignorant Claimant

micro_segments.title: The Tension: Why Would Migo Not Apply—I Could Have Remained Silent
Rav's Response: The Case Involves a Katan (Ignorant Minor-Like Claimant)
Gemara's Objection: A Katan's Claim Has No Halachic Force, So How Does It Override Migo?
Resolution: The Ignorant Adult Is Like a Katan; Chachamim Call It Meshiv Aveida, Rabbi Eliezer Ben Yaakov Calls It Modeh B'Miktzat
Final Gemara Aporia: Is This a Case of Other's Claim or One's Own Admission?
